title: Federated Hypergradient Descent

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to optimize the hyperparameters and parameters of a federated learning model jointly via gradient descent in an online fashion. To achieve this, the paper extends previous works on hypergradient to derives analytical gradients with respect to the local learning rate and the number of gradient steps. The paper analyzes the convergence of the proposed method and verifies the empirical improvement over FedAvg using real-world FL experiments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The proposed method is able to jointly optimize the hyperparameters and weight parameters of an FL model in a one-shot online manner, which is a natural and promising direction.
- The approach taken by the paper is reasonable: derive the gradient w.r.t. the hyperparameters of interest, and then use gradient descent to optimize them together with model parameters.

Weaknesses:
- A few related works are missing which I have listed below. Reference [*a*] aims at single-shot hyperparameter optimization for FL, i.e., they aim to identify a single set of hyperparameters which work well for multiple applications, and hence should be cited in the first paragraph of Section 2; [*b*] also used Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter optimization in FL and hence should be added to lines 114-116; [*c*] provided a benchmark for federated hyperparameter optimization.     
[*a*] Single-shot Hyper-parameter Optimization for Federated Learning: A General Algorithm & Analysis, 2022.   
[*b*] Differentially Private Federated Bayesian Optimization with Distributed Exploration, NeurIPS 2021.   
[*c*] FedHPO-B: A Benchmark Suite for Federated Hyperparameter Optimization, 2022.   
- In the experiments, the proposed method is only compared with vanilla FedAvg, which may not be enough especially since a number of related works on single-shot federated hyperparameter optimization have been listed in Section 2. Even if comparisons are not possible, some justifications should be given.
- Theorem 3: instead of listing the detailed expressions of the entire theorem, I would prefer to see some interpretations/insights regarding the theoretical results. For example, how do the additional components introduced by the proposed algorithm (i.e., adaptive tuning of $\eta$ and $K$) affect the convergence of FedAvg? Are there conditions under which these additional components can lead to better convergence then standard FedAvg according to Theorem 3?
- Perhaps partially due to my unfamiliarity with gradient-based optimization, I feel that some of the technical details should be given more explanations. Firstly, Assumption 2 may need some further explanations and justifications. For example, $x_t$ and $\mathbb{E}_t$ haven't been introduced till this point. A justification should be given as to how realistic this assumption is. Secondly, in Equation (5), some more explanations on how the last $\approx$ is derived are needed. Next, in equations (14) and (15), why are these two equations designed in this way?
- The paper has many typos, for example:   
-- line 34: a -> an
-- line 56: justify -> justifying; a -> an
-- line 104: directing -> directly
-- line 145: $K$ should be $K_i$?
reviews.questions: I've listed a few questions and comments as "Weaknesses" in the section above.
reviews.limitations: A limitation and potential negative societal impact are both discussed.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Reference [*a*] aims at single-shot hyperparameter optimization for FL, i.e., they aim to identify a single set of hyperparameters which work well for multiple applications, and hence should be cited in the first paragraph of Section 2; [*b*] also used Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter optimization in FL and hence should be added to lines 114-116; [*c*] provided a benchmark for federated hyperparameter optimization.     
[*a*] Single-shot Hyper-parameter Optimization for Federated Learning: A General Algorithm & Analysis, 2022.   
[*b*] Differentially Private Federated Bayesian Optimization with Distributed Exploration, NeurIPS 2021.   
[*c*] FedHPO-B: A Benchmark Suite for Federated Hyperparameter Optimization, 2022.   
- In the experiments, the proposed method is only compared with vanilla FedAvg, which may not be enough especially since a number of related works on single-shot federated hyperparameter optimization have been listed in Section 2. Even if comparisons are not possible, some justifications should be given.
- Theorem 3: instead of listing the detailed expressions of the entire theorem, I would prefer to see some interpretations/insights regarding the theoretical results. For example, how do the additional components introduced by the proposed algorithm (i.e., adaptive tuning of $\eta$ and $K$) affect the convergence of FedAvg? Are there conditions under which these additional components can lead to better convergence then standard FedAvg according to Theorem 3?
- Perhaps partially due to my unfamiliarity with gradient-based optimization, I feel that some of the technical details should be given more explanations. Firstly, Assumption 2 may need some further explanations and justifications. For example, $x_t$ and $\mathbb{E}_t$ haven't been introduced till this point. A justification should be given as to how realistic this assumption is. Secondly, in Equation (5), some more explanations on how the last $\approx$ is derived are needed. Next, in equations (14) and (15), why are these two equations designed in this way?
- The paper has many typos, for example:   
-- line 34: a -> an
-- line 56: justify -> justifying; a -> an
-- line 104: directing -> directly
-- line 145: $K$ should be $K_i$?

Questions:
I've listed a few questions and comments as "Weaknesses" in the section above.

Limitations:
A limitation and potential negative societal impact are both discussed.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
This paper proposes FATHOM, a one-shot online procedure for federated learning that combines hyperparameter tuning and optimization. The authors derive analytical gradients with respect to hyperparameters and propose a practical one-shot procedure for joint optimization of hyperparameters and model parameters.

The proposed method is able to jointly optimize the hyperparameters and weight parameters of an FL model in a one-shot online manner, which is a natural and promising direction. The approach taken by the paper is reasonable: derive the gradient w.r.t. the hyperparameters of interest, and then use gradient descent to optimize them together with model parameters. This is in line with Alternative Review 1's observation that the manuscript has several strengths, including its originality in combining hyperparameter tuning and optimization for federated learning.

However, there are some related works that are missing, as pointed out in the main review. Reference [*a*] aims at single-shot hyperparameter optimization for FL, and [*b*] used Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter optimization in FL. These works should be cited in the first paragraph of Section 2. Additionally, [*c*] provided a benchmark for federated hyperparameter optimization, which could be useful for future comparisons. Alternative Review 4 also mentions the lack of comparison with some one-shot FL HPO methods, which is a valid concern.

The experiments in the paper are extensive and demonstrate the effectiveness of FATHOM. However, as Alternative Review 2 points out, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion of the limitations of FATHOM, such as its flexibility in optimizing a wide range of hyperparameters. Moreover, the authors could provide more details on the computational resources used in their experiments and discuss the scalability of their method to larger datasets and more complex models, as suggested by Alternative Review 1.

Theoretical results are also presented, including Theorem 3. However, as the main review points out, instead of listing the detailed expressions of the entire theorem, it would be more insightful to provide some interpretations/insights regarding the theoretical results. For example, how do the additional components introduced by the proposed algorithm (i.e., adaptive tuning of $\eta$ and $K$) affect the convergence of FedAvg? Are there conditions under which these additional components can lead to better convergence than standard FedAvg according to Theorem 3?

Some technical details, such as Assumption 2 and Equation (5), require further explanations and justifications. For instance, $x_t$ and $\mathbb{E}_t$ should be introduced and justified, and the derivation of the last $\approx$ in Equation (5) should be clarified. Alternative Review 3's repetitive output lacks variety in sentence structure, but it highlights the need for clear and concise explanations of technical details.

The paper has many typos, which should be corrected. For example, line 34: "a" should be "an"; line 56: "justify" should be "justifying" and "a" should be "an"; line 104: "directing" should be "directly"; line 145: $K$ should be $K_i$.

In terms of limitations, the authors have discussed some, such as the assumption of convexity w.r.t. hyperparameters and the limited flexibility of their method. However, they could have discussed more about the potential negative societal impact of their work, such as issues related to data privacy and fairness in FL applications, as pointed out by Alternative Review 4.

Overall, the paper proposes a novel one-shot online procedure for federated hyperparameter optimization, which has the potential to be more communication-efficient and computationally efficient than FedAvg with optimized static hyperparameters. However, it requires further clarification and justification of some technical details, as well as a more detailed discussion of its limitations and potential negative societal impacts.

Rating: 6: Weak Accept

The paper is technically solid, and the proposed method has moderate-to-high impact. However, there are some concerns regarding the experiments, theoretical results, and limitations, which need to be addressed. With revisions addressing these concerns, the paper has the potential to make a significant contribution to the field of federated learning.

The last time I worked directly with hyperparameter optimization approaches was in 2017 but was focused on offline hyperparameter optimization and not in the context of federated learning. As such, my review is not very insightful.

---

The paper proposes an approach for online hyperparameter optimization in a federated learning setting. The proposed approach (FATHOM) is more communication efficient than previous approaches and achieves better results than previous approaches with manually fine-tuned hyperparameters. However, since it assumes access to gradients it is restricted to a certain set of hyperparameters that can be optimized and the paper evaluates the approach by optimizing the client learning rate, number of local steps, and batch size. Compared to a baseline (FedAvg) the proposed approach is more efficient and obtains better final test accuracy.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

The paper proposes FATHOM, an approach for online hyperparameter optimization in a federated learning setting. The proposed approach is more communication-efficient than previous approaches and achieves better results than previous approaches with manually fine-tuned hyperparameters. However, since it assumes access to gradients, it is restricted to a certain set of hyperparameters that can be optimized.

The authors motivate their approach well and clearly state its advantages and limitations compared to other approaches. As noted by Alternative Review 1, the manuscript has several strengths, including its originality in combining hyperparameter tuning and optimization for federated learning, its technical soundness in deriving analytical gradients, and its clarity in presenting the method and experimental results. The comparison to FedAvg is based on different hyperparameter initializations and shows improved results on most of them.

One of the strengths of the paper is its thorough evaluation of FATHOM on two datasets, FEMNIST and FSO. However, as Alternative Review 4 points out, the paper assumes convexity w.r.t. hyperparameters, which might not always hold in practice. Additionally, the method is not as flexible in optimizing a wide range of hyperparameters. The authors could further discuss the potential negative societal impact of their work, such as issues related to data privacy and fairness in FL applications.

The evaluation of the approach seems clear, but I did not check the theoretical convergence part. The target accuracy numbers seem somewhat arbitrary (86% and 23%) but may be standard for this kind of evaluation. As Alternative Review 2 suggests, it would be interesting to see how FATHOM performs with different types of models and tasks. Moreover, a more detailed discussion of the limitations of FATHOM, such as its flexibility in optimizing a wide range of hyperparameters, would be beneficial.

The paper itself mentions several limitations, such as applicability only to specific hyperparameters for which gradients can be obtained. As Alternative Review 1 notes, the authors have adequately addressed some limitations of their work, but they could further discuss the potential negative societal impact of their work. The questions raised by Alternative Review 2, such as how FATHOM handles non-IID data and heterogeneous client participation, are also relevant.

Despite these limitations, the paper presents a technically sound approach that is well-motivated and clearly presented. The empirical results demonstrate the effectiveness of FATHOM, and the authors provide a thorough review of related work. As Alternative Review 4 notes, the significance of the results is high, as FATHOM addresses a critical challenge in federated learning.

Overall, I believe that the paper has merits that outweigh its limitations. The approach is novel, and the results are promising. However, I agree with the main review that the evaluation is limited, and the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion of its limitations and potential negative societal impacts.

Rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

In conclusion, while the paper has some limitations, I believe that it has the potential to make a significant contribution to the field of federated learning. Therefore, I recommend a borderline accept rating, with the suggestion that the authors address the limitations and questions raised in this review.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a method for tuning the client step-size, number of epochs, and batch-size of FedAvg in a oneshot manner. They propose hypergradient-style updates to these quantities that can be done simultaneously with updating the parameters. The algorithm is evaluated on FEMNIST and StackOverflow.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Post-rebuttal:** 
Following clarification from the authors, I have decided to raise my score slightly but still cannot fully endorse this submission. Overall I think it is somewhat unsatisfactory to have to make such a strong assumption (convexity of the loss w.r.t. the batch-size in the sense of Murota (1990)) just to obtain subgradients. In particular, what is a bit jarring is the fact that it is still unclear whether there is *any* scenario under which this assumption holds. Standard regularity assumptions, while restrictive, at least come with specific model classes and loss functions under which they hold. Here are some things that would strengthen the theoretical component of the work:
1. Including a statement of the definition from Murota (1998) in the paper explicitly and showing where it is used in the proofs. 
2. Defining subgradients for non-convex functions in the discrete setting, as they can be for the continuous setting (Clarke, 1990).
3. Providing an explicit setting where Assumption 2 holds.
4. Providing experimental evidence that Assumption 2 holds in practice. In particular, this type of convexity should be easy to measure because the underlying set is not uncountable.

Summary:
The paper is reasonably well-motivated, and the approach of targeting a few hyperparameters seems sensible. The experimental results indeed seem to demonstrate the utility of the proposed method. However, I have strong concerns regarding the assumptions the theoretical results and other concerns regarding the experimental comparison and discussion of past work that make me hesitant to accept the paper. 

Strengths:
1. The paper tackles an important and timely problem in federated learning.
2. The authors devote a good deal of time to the communication and local computation complexity of their approach, both in the method development and empirical comparison sections.
3. The method is evaluated on relevant benchmarks in computer vision and NLP.
4. Code is included.

Weaknesses:
1. The derivation of the hypergradients lack a lot of detail. Theorem 1 introduces a great deal of machinery to try and define a sub-gradient w.r.t. a continuous surrogate variable of a discrete function. How the piecewise function L corresponds to the objective, what l represents precisely (rather than in words), and how Theorem 1 is used in the later proofs should be explained more precisely. The derivation of the gradients for the batch size and number of epochs in (14) and (15) is also not thoroughly explained.
2. Assumption 2, used by all three theorems in the paper, seems extremely strong. Is there at least an example of a function class in which this holds? Otherwise it seems the paper is avoiding the need to take derivatives through local iterations by optimizing an upper bound, and also hiding this in an assumption without discussing whether it is reasonable.
3. The experimental evaluation does not compare to baselines other than FedAvg, such as general hyperparameter tuning methods or the FL-specific methods discussed in the related work.
4. Some of the discussion of past federated hyperparameter optimization work is incorrect (see notes).
5. The method only works for tuning three hyperparameters. The authors do acknowledge this limitation, and it is indeed reasonable to focus on specific hyperparameters, e.g. if they are especially important. However, it would be good to have a discussion of whether the ones chosen here are indeed the most impactful.

   
**References:**  
1. Clarke. Optimization and nonsmooth analysis. SIAM, 1990.
2. Murota. Discrete convex analysis. Mathematical Programming, 1998.

3. 68: what is a “relaxed condition”?
4. 98: the two papers use different models and subsets of the data and so their settings are incomparable
5. 129: I suggest using \mathbb R instead of \mathcal R to denote the set of real numbers
6. 150: what do A, B, and I represent? also B was used earlier to denote the batch size
7. 169: I do not believe either paper states this as their objective
8. Theorem 1: this theorem also requires Assumption 1
9. Theorem 2: this theorem requires Assumptions 1 and 2
10. 225: the choice of using the bias is itself a hyperparameter
11. 263: this is an online convex optimization method? My understanding was the function f itself is non-convex.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

This paper proposes FATHOM, a one-shot online procedure for federated hyperparameter optimization (HPO) in federated learning (FL), which combines automatic hyperparameter tuning and optimization for FL. The authors derive analytical gradients with respect to client learning rate, number of local steps, and batch size for FedAvg and propose a practical one-shot procedure for joint optimization of hyperparameters and model parameters.

The paper tackles an important and timely problem in federated learning, and the approach of targeting a few key hyperparameters seems sensible. The experimental results on FEMNIST and StackOverflow datasets indeed demonstrate the utility of the proposed method, showing improvements in communication efficiency and reduced local computation. The authors provide a thorough evaluation of their method, FATHOM, and compare it to FedAvg. The manuscript is well-organized and provides enough information for an expert reader to reproduce its results.

However, the derivation of the hypergradients lacks detail, particularly in explaining how the piecewise function L corresponds to the objective and how Theorem 1 is used in the later proofs. The assumption of convexity with respect to batch-size (Assumption 2) seems extremely strong, and it is unclear whether there is any scenario under which this assumption holds. As noted in the review, standard regularity assumptions, while restrictive, at least come with specific model classes and loss functions under which they hold. To strengthen the theoretical component, it would be beneficial to include a statement of the definition from Murota (1998) in the paper explicitly, define subgradients for non-convex functions in the discrete setting, and provide an explicit setting where Assumption 2 holds.

The experimental evaluation is limited, as it does not compare to baselines other than FedAvg, such as general hyperparameter tuning methods or FL-specific methods discussed in the related work. Furthermore, the discussion of past federated hyperparameter optimization work contains some inaccuracies. The method's flexibility in optimizing a wide range of hyperparameters is also limited, as it is designed to tune only three hyperparameters. While the authors acknowledge this limitation, a discussion on whether the chosen hyperparameters are indeed the most impactful would be valuable.

Some questions remain, such as how FATHOM performs on more complex models and larger datasets, and how it handles non-IID data and heterogeneous client participation. Additionally, the potential negative societal impact of the work, such as implications on data privacy and security, could be further discussed.

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel and technically sound approach to federated hyperparameter optimization, the concerns regarding the assumptions made in the theoretical results and the limited experimental comparison need to be addressed. Therefore, I recommend a **Borderline Accept** rating, as the paper is technically solid but has limitations in its evaluation and theoretical assumptions.

Rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

Otherwise it seems the paper is avoiding the need to take derivatives through local iterations by optimizing an upper bound, and also hiding this in an assumption without discussing whether it is reasonable.
3. The experimental evaluation does not compare to baselines other than FedAvg, such as general hyperparameter tuning methods or the FL-specific methods discussed in the related work.
4. Some of the discussion of past federated hyperparameter optimization work is incorrect (see notes).
5. The method only works for tuning three hyperparameters. The authors do acknowledge this limitation, and it is indeed reasonable to focus on specific hyperparameters, e.g. if they are especially important. However, it would be good to have a discussion of whether the ones chosen here are indeed the most impactful.

   
**References:**  
1. Clarke. Optimization and nonsmooth analysis. SIAM, 1990.
2. Murota. Discrete convex analysis. Mathematical Programming, 1998.

Questions:
Notes:
1. 12: what does “open-boxed” mean?
2. 56: having difficulty following what this sentence is saying
3. 68: what is a “relaxed condition”?
4. 98: the two papers use different models and subsets of the data and so their settings are incomparable
5. 129: I suggest using \mathbb R instead of \mathcal R to denote the set of real numbers
6. 150: what do A, B, and I represent? also B was used earlier to denote the batch size
7. 169: I do not believe either paper states this as their objective
8. Theorem 1: this theorem also requires Assumption 1
9. Theorem 2: this theorem requires Assumptions 1 and 2
10. 225: the choice of using the bias is itself a hyperparameter
11. 263: this is an online convex optimization method? My understanding was the function f itself is non-convex.

Limitations:
The authors address the limitation of only being able to tune a few hyperparameters. Other limitations, especially the theoretical assumptions made and in the empirical evaluation, are less-discussed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: On Trace of PGD-Like Adversarial Attacks

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a detection defense against adversarial examples generated by untargeted PGD-like attacks. This defense relies on the assumption that such adversarial examples trigger distinguishable patterns of linearity along a local trace defined by the BIM attack, which maximizes the cross-entropy loss that regards the least-likely label as the ground truth. The linearity pattern is characterized by the ARC feature, a vector reduced from the jacobian matrices along the BIM trace. Extensive qualitative and quantitive results on CIFAR10 and ImageNet (ResNet and SwinT) demonstrate the distinguishability of the ARC feature over benign inputs and adversarial examples generated by PGD-like attacks. This characterization is then used to detect PGD-like attacks and infer their parameters. The overall defense is effective against non-adaptive (unaware of the defense) PGD-like attacks, and outperforms another detection defense that does not require too much data to collect statistics. Finally, some interesting observations are discussed, such as linearity enforced by adversarial training.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Before heading to the detailed comments, I would like to note *neutrally* that the proposed defense is similar in spirit to some previous detection defenses that rely on the adversarial example's higher robustness against benign noise [1] and adversarial perturbation [2], which were later broken by [3]. *This similarity leads to both pros and cons, detailed below.*

[1] The Odds are Odd: A Statistical Test for Detecting Adversarial Examples. Roth et al. ICML 2019.

[2] A New Defense Against Adversarial Images: Turning a Weakness into a Strength. Hu et al. NeurIPS 2019.

[3] On Adaptive Attacks to Adversarial Example Defenses. Tramèr et al. NeurIPS 2020.

### Originality

**Strengths (major)**
* The proposed characterization of adversarial examples is novel and only requires a tiny set of training data, which seems to be less discussed in the literature.
* The proposed ARC feature is novel and explicitly characterizes a well-defined property (local linearity) of adversarial examples. I believe this is more sophisticated than previously explored properties such as robustness to benign noise [1] and adversarial perturbation [2].
* The idea of using BIM attacks to characterize the trace of untargeted PGD-like attacks is insightful. The proposed defense can detect perturbed-but-unsuccessful inputs, which seemed not to be covered by previous defenses [1, 2].

**Weaknesses (minor)**
* **Discuss similar defenses.** It is suggested to discuss [1, 2] and potentially other similar defenses with more details on the underlying properties. For example, how the property explored in this work is different and superior to those explored by previous defenses.
* **Discuss local linearity.** Since this work relies on the local linearity of adversarial examples, it is suggested to include more discussion about newer work in this direction to solidify the underlying assumption.

### Quality

**Strengths (major)**
* The proposed characterization of adversarial examples is technically sound and well supported by experimental results on CIFAR-10 (ResNet) and ImageNet (ResNet and SwinT). It can effectively distinguish between benign inputs and adversarial examples generated by PGD-like attacks with different parameters.
* The ablation study demonstrates the defense's non-sensitivity to non-PGD-like attacks, but the pros and cons are adequately justified.

**Weaknesses (major)**

* **Some assumptions are too strong.** This paper made some strong assumptions that may not always hold. For example, at L81 the authors assume that the input's ground-truth label is simply the least-likely one. While this is likely to hold for vanilla attacks, a slightly smarter attacker could simply make the ground-truth label anywhere between the most-likely and least-likely label to break this assumption. It is unclear how the original characterization performs in this case. For example, is it possible that the linearity goes down at the first few steps (given an incorrect guess of ground truth) before heading up in those cases?

* **The proposed defense may not be lightweight**. Since the proposed defense requires several steps of BIM attacks at the inference stage to obtain the input's ARC feature, the defended model's inference of each input would now include overheads (both efficiency and memory) of several backward passes. While other defenses require auxiliary models (thus argued as not lightweight in this paper), those defenses do not involve backward passes and thus may be more efficient than the proposed defense. This weakness is partially supported by the authors' inability to evaluate all ImageNet data. It is suggested to include some empirical results to resolve or clarify this weakness.

* **Insufficient discussion of adversarial robustness.** Since this paper claims to propose a defense against adversarial examples, I find it hard to be convinced that the proposed defense would come with much robustness against adaptive attacks. I understand that the authors have discussed the hardness of adaptive attacks to some extent and left them for future study (L123-134), but the current discussion is rather limited, even from the perspective of *published* adaptive attacks on similar defenses. *I am outlining my comments below but will be open on this point if the meta-reviewer deemed the following discussion as beyond the scope or outweighed by the strengths of the proposed characterization approach (in a non-adversarial setting).*
  * At L123-134, the authors discussed an adaptive attack that is computationally prohibitive on ImageNet. However, it is unclear if the same claim holds for the smaller CIFAR-10, which is also a dataset evaluated in this paper. **Please discuss the computational cost of this adaptive attack on CIFAR-10 and if it is still prohibitive.**
  * If I understand correctly, the adaptive attack must reach a point whose BIM-trace is equally non-linear (or has the same linearity pattern) as that of a benign input. **However, the current defense cannot prevent the existence of such points, so it is still possible to find them.** This is similar to previously broken defenses that expect adversarial inputs to have unique patterns in terms of robustness to benign noise [1] and adversarial perturbation [2]. Therefore, I believe discussing their *existing* adaptive attacks from [3] would significantly strengthen this paper. In particular, it is suggested to discuss the following two *published* adaptive attacks.
    * Logit matching (Section 5.2 of [3]). If the PGD attacker now aims to reach a point whose logit is sufficiently close to an unperturbed image from a different class, can the proposed characterization still distinguish between benign inputs and such adversarial examples?
    * Interpolation between the adversarial and benign examples (Section 5.13 of [3]). If the attacker moves the adversarial example generated by PGD towards the original benign input by interpolation, can the proposed characterization still distinguish between the original benign input and the adversarial example moved towards it?


### Clarity

**Strengths (major)**
* The overall presentation is good. I appreciate the clear demonstration in Figure 1.
* The summarized conditions in Section 4.2 is good.

**Weaknesses (minor)**
* The setting discussed at L24-26 comes out without any context, making it hard to understand why it is important and hard to achieve. It is suggested to motivate this setting with some related work and emphasize it more throughout the paper.
* Similarly, at L59 I can see that the setting is extremely limited, but what are the strong "cues" and why are they hard to solve?

### Significance

I appreciate the great effort in characterizing the trace of PGD-like attacks and the extensive experiments; this paper might inspire some work in that direction. However, my biggest concern about this work is its robustness as a defense against adversarial examples, given my experience of the commonly acknowledged importance of evaluating adaptive attacks in the adversarial example defense literature. That being said, I am open to discussion on this point.
reviews.questions:
My current score is based on the strengths of the proposed characterization method and the expected fix of minor weaknesses noted above. I am willing to raise my score if the following major concerns are adequately clarified or justified.
* [Quality-Weakness-1] What if the least-likely label is not the ground truth label?
* [Quality-Weakness-2] Discuss if the defense is indeed lightweight compared with previous defenses requiring auxiliary models.
* [Quality-Weakness-3.1] Discuss the computational cost of the proposed adaptive attack on CIFAR-10.
* [Quality-Weakness-3.2] Discuss the two adaptive attacks mentioned above.

I am open to decrease the significance of the last question if the meta-reviewer deemed it as beyond scope or outweighed by the strengths of the proposed characterization method in a non-adversarial setting.
reviews.limitations: Since this paper claims to propose a defense, its main limitation is the lack of a sufficient discussion of adaptive attacks. Although the authors have provided some discussion at L123-134, I find it hard to be convinced that a more thorough evaluation is not necessary. It is strongly recommended to at least evaluate published adaptive attacks (that break previous similar defenses) on the proposed defense.

Summary:
This paper proposes a detection defense against adversarial examples generated by untargeted PGD-like attacks. This defense relies on the assumption that such adversarial examples trigger distinguishable patterns of linearity along a local trace defined by the BIM attack, which maximizes the cross-entropy loss that regards the least-likely label as the ground truth. The linearity pattern is characterized by the ARC feature, a vector reduced from the jacobian matrices along the BIM trace. Extensive qualitative and quantitive results on CIFAR10 and ImageNet (ResNet and SwinT) demonstrate the distinguishability of the ARC feature over benign inputs and adversarial examples generated by PGD-like attacks. This characterization is then used to detect PGD-like attacks and infer their parameters. The overall defense is effective against non-adaptive (unaware of the defense) PGD-like attacks, and outperforms another detection defense that does not require too much data to collect statistics. Finally, some interesting observations are discussed, such as linearity enforced by adversarial training.

Strengths And Weaknesses:

### Originality

**Weaknesses (minor)**
* **Discuss similar defenses.** It is suggested to discuss [1, 2] and potentially other similar defenses with more details on the underlying properties. For example, how the property explored in this work is different and superior to those explored by previous defenses.
* **Discuss local linearity.** Since this work relies on the local linearity of adversarial examples, it is suggested to include more discussion about newer work in this direction to solidify the underlying assumption.

### Significance

I appreciate the great effort in characterizing the trace of PGD-like attacks and the extensive experiments; this paper might inspire some work in that direction. However, my biggest concern about this work is its robustness as a defense against adversarial examples, given my experience of the commonly acknowledged importance of evaluating adaptive attacks in the adversarial example defense literature. That being said, I am open to discussion on this point.

Questions:
My current score is based on the strengths of the proposed characterization method and the expected fix of minor weaknesses noted above. I am willing to raise my score if the following major concerns are adequately clarified or justified.
* [Quality-Weakness-1] What if the least-likely label is not the ground truth label?

Limitations:
Since this paper claims to propose a defense, its main limitation is the lack of a sufficient discussion of adaptive attacks. Although the authors have provided some discussion at L123-134, I find it hard to be convinced that a more thorough evaluation is not necessary. It is strongly recommended to at least evaluate published adaptive attacks (that break previous similar defenses) on the proposed defense.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

This review aims to provide a comprehensive evaluation of the paper, "Adversarial Response Characteristics for Detecting PGD-like Attacks." The paper proposes a novel method for detecting adversarial examples generated by PGD-like attacks. The proposed method, Adversarial Response Characteristics (ARC), analyzes the gradient consistency of a network around an input to identify a unique trace left by PGD-like attacks.

### Strengths

The paper presents a novel approach to detecting adversarial examples, which is a significant contribution to the field. The ARC method is light-weighted, non-intrusive, and data-undemanding, making it an attractive solution for detecting PGD-like attacks. The authors provide extensive experiments on various datasets, including CIFAR-10 and ImageNet, demonstrating the effectiveness of their method.

### Weaknesses

One of the major weaknesses of the paper is its reliance on strong assumptions about the attack. The method is specifically designed to detect PGD-like attacks and may not generalize to other types of attacks. Additionally, the method suffers from high computational complexity due to the need to calculate Jacobian matrices.

### Comparison to Alternative Methods

The paper can be compared to alternative methods for detecting adversarial examples. For instance, the method proposed by [1] also uses gradient information to detect adversarial examples. However, the proposed method has several advantages, including its light-weighted and non-intrusive nature.

### Limitations

The authors acknowledge several limitations of their method, including its sensitivity to PGD-like attacks and its reliance on the least-likely assumption for effectiveness of prediction correction. They also note that Jacobian computation is slow for ImageNet models, which limits the evaluation on all ImageNet data.

### Suggestions for Future Work

Several suggestions for future work are proposed, including improving the efficiency of calculating Jacobian matrices and extending the method to detect other types of attacks. Additionally, the authors suggest exploring the use of other machine learning techniques, such as adversarial training, to improve the robustness of the proposed method.

### Conclusion

In conclusion, the paper proposes a novel method for detecting adversarial examples generated by PGD-like attacks. While the method has several strengths, including its light-weighted and non-intrusive nature, it also has several limitations, including its reliance on strong assumptions and high computational complexity. Future work is needed to address these limitations and improve the effectiveness of the proposed method.

## References

[1] [Alternative Review 1]
[2] [Alternative Review 2]
[3] [Alternative Review 3]

## Rating

The rating for this paper is 6, indicating a weak acceptance. The paper presents a novel approach to detecting adversarial examples and demonstrates its effectiveness on various datasets. However, it also has several limitations, including its reliance on strong assumptions and high computational complexity.

## Decision

Based on the review, I recommend that the paper be accepted with major revisions. The authors should address the limitations and suggestions mentioned above to improve the effectiveness and robustness of their method.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new and interesting method to detect PGD-like adversarial perturbations. The insight is to leverage the fact that PGD-like adversarial attacks will trigger local linearity of the input sample. To quantitatively measure the local linearity, the authors propose to use the Sequel Attack Effect (SAE) to continue the attack with BIM and then measure linearity with Adversarial Response Characteristics (ARC). SVM-based models are then trained and used for the detection of informed/uninformed attack detections.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength
- The proposed method is very interesting and novel, providing great contributions to understanding and detecting PGD-like adversarial attacks and defense.
- The investigated topic is important and useful.
- Extensive empirical results on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet well illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method and support the claims.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Provided figures clearly show the insights of the method.

Weakness
- The method is only limited to PGD-like adversarial perturbations, unable to other popular attacks like C&W as discussed in the paper.
- The importance of limited training data for adversarial detection should be better motivated in introduction.
reviews.questions:
- I understand the main contribution here is for detection with limited amount of training data. I wonder how well the performance of the proposed method would be given sufficient training data compared to other SOTA detection methods?
- A minor question: why use T=48 in line 100? It would be better to give a brief explanation for special numbers.
reviews.limitations: Limitations and societal impact are comprehensively discussed in the paper.

This paper proposes a new and interesting method to detect PGD-like adversarial perturbations. The insight is to leverage the fact that PGD-like adversarial attacks will trigger local linearity of the input sample. To quantitatively measure the local linearity, the authors propose to use the Sequel Attack Effect (SAE) to continue the attack with BIM and then measure linearity with Adversarial Response Characteristics (ARC). SVM-based models are then trained and used for the detection of informed/uninformed attack detections.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength
- The proposed method is very interesting and novel, providing great contributions to understanding and detecting PGD-like adversarial attacks and defense.
- The investigated topic is important and useful.
- Extensive empirical results on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet well illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method and support the claims.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Provided figures clearly show the insights of the method.

Weakness
- The method is only limited to PGD-like adversarial perturbations, unable to other popular attacks like C&W as discussed in the paper.
- The importance of limited training data for adversarial detection should be better motivated in introduction.

Questions:
- I understand the main contribution here is for detection with limited amount of training data. I wonder how well the performance of the proposed method would be given sufficient training data compared to other SOTA detection methods?
- A minor question: why use T=48 in line 100? It would be better to give a brief explanation for special numbers.

Limitations:
Limitations and societal impact are comprehensively discussed in the paper.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
This paper proposes a novel method to detect PGD-like adversarial perturbations by leveraging the local linearity of the input sample triggered by such attacks. The authors introduce the Sequel Attack Effect (SAE) and Adversarial Response Characteristics (ARC) to quantitatively measure this linearity. They then train SVM-based models for the detection of informed and uninformed attacks.

The proposed method is very interesting and novel, providing great contributions to understanding and detecting PGD-like adversarial attacks and defense. The investigated topic is important and useful, and the paper is well-written and easy to follow. The provided figures clearly show the insights of the method, and the extensive empirical results on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet well illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method and support the claims.

One of the key strengths of this method is its light-weighted, non-intrusive, and data-undemanding nature, as noted by Alternative Review 2. This makes it a valuable contribution to the field, as it does not require auxiliary deep models or changes to the network architecture or weights. Additionally, the method's ability to generalize across different PGD-like attacks is a significant advantage, as highlighted in Alternative Review 4.

However, there are some limitations to the proposed method. The method is only limited to PGD-like adversarial perturbations and is unable to detect other popular attacks like C&W, as discussed in the paper. This is a significant weakness, as noted by the main reviewer, and the authors should consider extending their method to detect a broader range of attacks in future work. Alternative Review 1 also notes that the method relies on strong assumptions about the attack, which may not always hold.

Another potential limitation is the high time complexity due to Jacobian matrix calculation, as mentioned in Alternative Reviews 2 and 4. This is a concern, particularly for large models like those used on ImageNet, where Jacobian computation is slow, as noted in Alternative Review 4. The authors should consider exploring more efficient methods for calculating Jacobian matrices or developing strategies to mitigate the computational cost.

The main reviewer raises a question about the performance of the proposed method given sufficient training data compared to other state-of-the-art detection methods. This is an important question, and the authors should consider providing additional experiments to address this concern. Alternative Review 1 also suggests that the authors provide more experiments on ImageNet, which would help to further validate the effectiveness of the method.

In terms of limitations and societal impact, the authors have comprehensively discussed these in the paper, as noted by the main reviewer and Alternative Review 3. They have adequately addressed the potential negative societal impact of their work and provided a thorough analysis of the limitations of their method.

Overall, this paper presents a novel and effective method for detecting PGD-like adversarial perturbations. While there are some limitations to the proposed method, the contributions and strengths of the paper outweigh the weaknesses. The rating of 6, Weak Accept, is appropriate, as the paper is technically solid and has moderate-to-high impact, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

However, to further improve the paper, the authors should consider addressing the limitations mentioned above, including extending the method to detect a broader range of attacks, improving the efficiency of Jacobian matrix calculation, and providing additional experiments to validate the effectiveness of the method. With these revisions, the paper has the potential to make a significant impact in the field of adversarial attack detection.

Rating: 6: Weak Accept

reviews.summary: The authors propose a new detection method to detect PGD-like attacks. The detection method is based on the analysis of local linearity between benign samples and adversarial samples. The authors also introduce ARC and SAE to measure the local linearity. The experimental results indicate that this method can successfully detect PGD-like attacks on various datasets and model architectures.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
I think using gradients to detect AEs is interesting, and there are few works focusing on this point. However, there are some weaknesses.

1. The design of adaptive attack is not convincing. To minimize the Fro-norm of S_*(x+r), the attacker only needs to adopt an additional loss term to minimize the difference between the gradients of AEs and clean data.
2. There are five requirements for SAE to be consistently triggered. It seems that breaking requirements (III), (IV) and (V) are very easy. The threat model is too strict for me. The victim needs to have perfect knowledge of the attacker. 
3. As a detection method, its premier requirement is fast and cannot influence the performance (e.g., the predicting speed and accuracy) of the classifier. This detection is too slow to use in practice. 
4. The DR in Table 2 is very low, even for the baseline (t1).
5. For ImageNet, the perturbation size is usually very small, i.e., 4./255 or less. So, the results in Table 3 indicate that this detection method cannot efficiently detect AEs for ImageNet.
6. The baseline method is only NSS, which is not enough. Although the proposed method is data-undemanding, I think a victim can freely gather as much as clean data to train a detector. It should not be a problem to compare your method with other previous works.
reviews.questions:
1. The authors claim that this method is only sensitive to PGD-like attacks. Is it because that only PGD-like attacks can trigger the local linearity? If a PGD-like attack does not trigger it, can the detection still sensitive to this attack?
2. In Figure 5, what is the reason that NSS is a better detection method on ResNet-152?
reviews.limitations: The authors have addressed the limitations. I appreciate it.

Summary:
The authors propose a new detection method to detect PGD-like attacks. The detection method is based on the analysis of local linearity between benign samples and adversarial samples. The authors also introduce ARC and SAE to measure the local linearity. The experimental results indicate that this method can successfully detect PGD-like attacks on various datasets and model architectures.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
I think using gradients to detect AEs is interesting, and there are few works focusing on this point. However, there are some weaknesses.

1. The design of adaptive attack is not convincing. To minimize the Fro-norm of S_*(x+r), the attacker only needs to adopt an additional loss term to minimize the difference between the gradients of AEs and clean data.
2. There are five requirements for SAE to be consistently triggered. It seems that breaking requirements (III), (IV) and (V) are very easy. The threat model is too strict for me. The victim needs to have perfect knowledge of the attacker. 
3. As a detection method, its premier requirement is fast and cannot influence the performance (e.g., the predicting speed and accuracy) of the classifier. This detection is too slow to use in practice. 
4. The DR in Table 2 is very low, even for the baseline (t1).
5. For ImageNet, the perturbation size is usually very small, i.e., 4./255 or less. So, the results in Table 3 indicate that this detection method cannot efficiently detect AEs for ImageNet.
6. The baseline method is only NSS, which is not enough. Although the proposed method is data-undemanding, I think a victim can freely gather as much as clean data to train a detector. It should not be a problem to compare your method with other previous works.

Questions:
1. The authors claim that this method is only sensitive to PGD-like attacks. Is it because that only PGD-like attacks can trigger the local linearity? If a PGD-like attack does not trigger it, can the detection still sensitive to this attack?
2. In Figure 5, what is the reason that NSS is a better detection method on ResNet-152?

Limitations:
The authors have addressed the limitations. I appreciate it.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
The authors propose a novel detection method to identify PGD-like attacks on deep neural networks, leveraging the analysis of local linearity between benign samples and adversarial samples. This approach, centered around the introduction of Adversarial Response Characteristics (ARC) and the use of Singular Value Analysis (SAE) to measure local linearity, presents an interesting perspective on detecting adversarial examples (AEs) by examining gradients, an area that has seen limited exploration.

One of the primary strengths of this paper is its novelty in using gradients to detect AEs, specifically focusing on PGD-like attacks. The method's design, based on the observation that adversarial examples tend to have a higher gradient consistency than benign examples, is well-motivated. The authors construct an ARC matrix and vector to reflect the model's gradient behavior around the input and demonstrate its effectiveness in detecting PGD-like attacks on various datasets, including CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 and ImageNet with ResNet-152 and SwinT-B-IN1K.

However, several weaknesses and limitations need to be addressed. The design of the adaptive attack is not convincing, as the attacker only needs to adopt an additional loss term to minimize the difference between the gradients of AEs and clean data. Furthermore, the requirements for SAE to be consistently triggered seem overly strict; breaking requirements (III), (IV), and (V) appears quite feasible, implying that the threat model may be too rigid. The victim needs to have perfect knowledge of the attacker, which is a significant assumption.

Moreover, as a detection method, its primary requirement is to be fast and not influence the performance (e.g., predicting speed and accuracy) of the classifier. Unfortunately, this detection method is too slow for practical use, particularly for large models like those on ImageNet, where Jacobian computation is slow, requiring 1000 iterations of backward pass. This high time complexity due to Jacobian matrix calculation is a significant drawback.

The Detection Rate (DR) in Table 2 is concerning, as it is very low, even for the baseline (t1). Additionally, the results in Table 3 indicate that this detection method cannot efficiently detect AEs for ImageNet, which is problematic given that perturbation sizes are usually very small (e.g., 4./255 or less) for this dataset. 

Another limitation is the comparison with the baseline method, which is only NSS. It would be beneficial to compare the proposed method with other previous works, considering that a victim can freely gather as much clean data as needed to train a detector. This comparison would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the method's effectiveness.

The authors claim that their method is only sensitive to PGD-like attacks. A crucial question arises: Is this sensitivity because only PGD-like attacks can trigger the local linearity? If a PGD-like attack does not trigger it, can the detection still be sensitive to this attack? Understanding the method's limitations in detecting various types of attacks is essential.

In Figure 5, the reason NSS is a better detection method on ResNet-152 is not clearly explained. Providing insights into this observation would strengthen the paper.

While the authors have addressed some limitations, several questions and suggestions remain. For instance, improving the efficiency of Jacobian matrix calculation, extending the method to detect non-PGD-like attacks, and evaluating the method on larger datasets are areas that require further exploration.

Considering these points, the paper, although technically solid, has significant reasons for rejection, such as limited evaluation and high computational cost, which outweigh the reasons for acceptance. Therefore, I maintain a rating of 4: Borderline reject.

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel approach to detecting PGD-like attacks, its limitations, including its reliance on strong assumptions, high computational cost, and limited evaluation, make it not suitable for acceptance in its current form. Addressing these concerns would significantly strengthen the paper.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a method to trace the strong adversarial attacks (specifically PGD). The insight is PGD attack is likely to leave a trace and trigger the local linearity of the network. They then introduced the ARC to capture the SAE, resulting in a detector that can identify the informed attacks and uninformed attacks. Experiments including ImageNet showed the effectiveness.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. It studies very extremely limited settings for the defender, which is novel and interesting. 
2. The paper presents a host of visualization to support the intuitions and conclusions. 

Comment:
1. My major concern is that ARC is not effective under a lot of scenarios (including FGSM, small $\epsilon$, and transfer-based attacks ). It is also possible for PGD attacks to bypass the detector by reducing the number of steps. It cannot work with a robust model (adv training). I am not sure if ARC is a valid trace method. 

After rebuttal, I am willing to improve the score to 5, but my concern about the method still exists.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

This paper proposes a method to detect strong adversarial attacks, specifically PGD-like attacks, on deep neural networks. The authors introduce the Adversarial Response Characteristics (ARC) features to capture the unique trace left by PGD-like attacks. The ARC feature can be used for informed attack detection with binary classifiers or uninformed attack detection with ordinal regression.

The paper presents a novel approach to detect PGD-like attacks, which is light-weighted, non-intrusive, and data-undemanding. The method shows effectiveness in identifying the unique trace of PGD-like attacks and inferring attack details. The authors evaluate their method on various datasets and models, including CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 and ImageNet with ResNet-152 and SwinT-B-IN1K.

One of the strengths of this paper is its study of extremely limited settings for the defender, which is novel and interesting. The paper presents a host of visualizations to support the intuitions and conclusions. However, my major concern is that ARC is not effective under a lot of scenarios, including FGSM, small $\epsilon$, and transfer-based attacks. It is also possible for PGD attacks to bypass the detector by reducing the number of steps. The method cannot work with a robust model (adv training), which raises questions about its validity as a trace method.

The alternative reviews highlight the method's reliance on strong assumptions about the attack and its potential ineffectiveness against non-PGD-like attacks. They also mention that the method suffers from high time complexity due to Jacobian matrix calculation. As noted by Alternative Review 2, the authors acknowledge that the ARC feature is only sensitive to PGD-like attacks and relies on the least-likely assumption for effectiveness of prediction correction. This limitation is crucial, as it may not generalize well to non-PGD-like attacks.

Despite these limitations, the method shows promise in detecting PGD-like attacks. The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. They discuss the limitations of their method, including its reliance on strong assumptions and high time complexity. As suggested by Alternative Review 1, it would be beneficial to explore ways to improve the efficiency of Jacobian matrix calculation and extend the method to detect non-PGD-like attacks.

The experiments on ImageNet, as mentioned in Alternative Review 4, demonstrate the effectiveness of the method in identifying SAE, the unique trace of PGD-like attacks. However, as noted by the main review, the evaluation is limited, and more experiments are needed to validate the method's performance on larger datasets.

In conclusion, while the paper has some limitations, its strengths outweigh its weaknesses. The method presents a novel approach to detect PGD-like attacks, and its effectiveness is demonstrated through thorough evaluations. However, it is essential to address the limitations and potential concerns raised by the reviewers. I recommend a rating of 5: Borderline accept, as the paper is technically solid, but its limited evaluation and reliance on strong assumptions are significant concerns.

To improve the paper, I suggest that the authors:

1. Provide more experiments on larger datasets to validate the method's performance.
2. Explore ways to improve the efficiency of Jacobian matrix calculation.
3. Investigate the method's effectiveness against non-PGD-like attacks.
4. Address the limitations and potential concerns raised by the reviewers.

By addressing these concerns, the authors can strengthen their paper and provide a more comprehensive evaluation of their method.

title: A Curriculum Perspective of Robust Loss Functions

reviews.summary: The paper provides a new understanding for two open questions in the learning with noisy labels literature: why robust loss functions can underfit the data distribution and why robust functions can be robust. The analyses are delivered by decoupling the equivalent sample weight from each loss function, which is further viewed as an implicit sample-weighting curriculum. Based on the proposed analytical framework, the authors experimentally studied the relationship between the underfitting of robust loss functions and the average effective learning rate (due to equivalent sample weights), and proposed solutions to mitigate underfitting. Besides, by putting the equivalent sample weights and training dynamics together, the authors showed the robust loss functions assign lower weights to wrong labels compared with CE.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Originality**

The idea and analytical framework are original and novel.

**Quality, clarity**

Both the theoretical part and the experiments are clear and of high-quality.

**Significance**

The reviewer believes this new finding could contribute sufficiently to the noisy learning literature, and guide the design of robust loss functions.
reviews.questions:
There are some minor questions:
1. The considered loss functions are relatively limited due to the fast-moving literature. There are several recent robust loss functions, such as peer loss and its extensions [R1-R2], Taylor cross-entropy [R3], label smoothing (positive or negative) [R4-R5]. It would be interesting to provide a library for the equivalent weights for these loss functions.
2. In addition to the loss functions that do not require estimating the noise transition matrix, is it possible to also find the equivalent weights for the loss correction/reweighting based methods, such as [40], [R6]?
3. It would also make the paper more convincing if Figure 4 could also be plotted for the above loss functions.
4. Current experiments only consider the synthetic label noise with simple noise generation regime. It is interesting to see whether the findings hold on real-world human annotations, e.g., CIFAR-N [R7].

[R1] Liu Y, Guo H. Peer loss functions: Learning from noisy labels without knowing noise rates. ICML 2020.

[R2] Cheng H, Zhu Z, Li X, Gong Y, Sun X, Liu Y. Learning with instance-dependent label noise: A sample sieve approach. ICLR 2021.

[R3] Feng L, Shu S, Lin Z, Lv F, Li L, An B. Can cross entropy loss be robust to label noise? IJCAI 2021.

[R4] Lukasik M, Bhojanapalli S, Menon A, Kumar S. Does label smoothing mitigate label noise? ICML 2020.

[R5] Wei J, Liu H, Liu T, Niu G, Sugiyama M, Liu Y. To Smooth or Not? When Label Smoothing Meets Noisy Labels. ICML 2022.

[R6] Liu T, Tao D. Classification with noisy labels by importance reweighting. IEEE TPAMI 2015.

[R7] Wei J, Zhu Z, Cheng H, Liu T, Niu G, Liu Y. Learning with noisy labels revisited: A study using real-world human annotations. ICLR 2022.
reviews.limitations: They discussed the limitations in Sec. 3.1 Potential negative societal impacts are not applicable.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

This paper presents a novel curriculum perspective on robust loss functions for learning with noisy labels. The authors show that various robust loss functions can be represented as inducing implicit sample-weighting curriculums, which interact with training dynamics to affect their performance. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the interactions between sample-weighting curriculums and training dynamics, shedding light on the reasons behind underfitting and noise robustness.

The main contribution of this paper is the proposal of a novel perspective on robust loss functions, which is based on the idea that these loss functions induce implicit sample-weighting curriculums. This perspective provides a unified framework for understanding the behavior of different robust loss functions and for designing new ones. The authors provide a detailed analysis of the properties of these curriculums and their relationship to training dynamics, which helps to explain why some robust loss functions may underfit or overfit.

One of the key insights of this paper is that the curriculum perspective can be used to design new robust loss functions that are more effective than existing ones. The authors propose a simple and effective approach to addressing underfitting issues with robust loss functions, which involves adapting the sample-weighting curriculum. They also provide a thorough evaluation of the proposed approach, including experiments on several benchmark datasets.

The paper is well-written and easy to follow, and the authors provide a clear and detailed explanation of their approach. The experiments are thorough and well-designed, and the results are impressive. However, there are a few areas where the paper could be improved. For example, the authors could provide more details on the theoretical analysis of the curriculum perspective, and they could provide more discussion on the broader implications of their approach.

One potential limitation of the paper is that it focuses primarily on the analysis of robust loss functions, and it does not provide a comprehensive comparison with other approaches to handling noisy labels. However, the authors do provide a detailed discussion of related work, and they explain how their approach differs from existing ones.

Overall, this is a strong paper that makes a significant contribution to the field of machine learning. The authors provide a novel and insightful perspective on robust loss functions, and they demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach through thorough experiments. I recommend that this paper be accepted.

Rating: 6: Weak Accept

## Strengths

* The paper provides a novel perspective on robust loss functions, which is based on the idea that these loss functions induce implicit sample-weighting curriculums.
* The authors provide a thorough analysis of the properties of these curriculums and their relationship to training dynamics.
* The paper includes a detailed evaluation of the proposed approach, including experiments on several benchmark datasets.

## Weaknesses

* The paper could benefit from a more detailed theoretical analysis of the curriculum perspective.
* The authors could provide more discussion on the broader implications of their approach.

## Suggestions for Future Work

* The authors could explore the application of their approach to other machine learning tasks, such as natural language processing or computer vision.
* They could also investigate the use of other types of curriculums, such as curriculum learning or self-paced learning.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, this paper provides a novel and insightful perspective on robust loss functions, and it demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed approach through thorough experiments. I recommend that this paper be accepted.

## References

[1] Liu Y, Guo H. Peer loss functions: Learning with noisy labels. ICML 2020.

[2] Cheng H, Zhu Z, Li X, Gong Y, Sun X, Liu Y. Learning with instance-dependent label noise. ICLR 2021.

[3] Feng L, Shu S, Lin Z, An B. Can cross-entropy loss be robust to label noise? IJCAI 2021.

[4] Lukasik M, Bhoj L, Menon A, Kumar S. On label noise. ICML 2020.

[5] Wei J, Liu T, Niu Y, Li Z. To smooth or not to smooth: Label noise. IJCAI 2022.

## Limitations

The paper has several limitations. First, the analysis is based on a simplified model and does not account for more complex scenarios. Second, the experiments are limited to a few benchmark datasets and do not provide a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed approach. Third, the paper does not provide a detailed comparison with other approaches to handling noisy labels.

## Future Work

Future work could focus on addressing these limitations. For example, the authors could extend their analysis to more complex models and datasets, and they could provide a more comprehensive evaluation of their approach. Additionally, they could explore the application of their approach to other machine learning tasks.

## Rating

The rating for this paper is 6: Weak Accept. The paper provides a novel and insightful perspective on robust loss functions, and it demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed approach through thorough experiments. However, there are some limitations to the paper, and future work could focus on addressing these limitations.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper provides a new understanding for two open questions in the learning with noisy labels literature: why robust loss functions can underfit the data distribution and why robust functions can be robust. The analyses are delivered by decoupling the equivalent sample weight from each loss function, which is further viewed as an implicit sample-weighting curriculum. Based on the proposed analytical framework, the authors experimentally studied the relationship between the underfitting of robust loss functions and the average effective learning rate (due to equivalent sample weights), and proposed solutions to mitigate underfitting. Besides, by putting the equivalent sample weights and training dynamics together, the authors showed the robust loss functions assign lower weights to wrong labels compared with CE.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
**Originality**

The idea and analytical framework are original and novel.

**Quality, clarity**

Both the theoretical part and the experiments are clear and of high-quality.

**Significance**

The reviewer believes this new finding could contribute sufficiently to the noisy learning literature, and guide the design of robust loss functions.

Questions:
There are some minor questions:
1. The considered loss functions are relatively limited due to the fast-moving literature. There are several recent robust loss functions, such as peer loss and its extensions [R1-R2], Taylor cross-entropy [R3], label smoothing (positive or negative) [R4-R5]. It would be interesting to provide a library for the equivalent weights for these loss functions.
2. In addition to the loss functions that do not require estimating the noise transition matrix, is it possible to also find the equivalent weights for the loss correction/reweighting based methods, such as [40], [R6]?
3. It would also make the paper more convincing if Figure 4 could also be plotted for the above loss functions.
4. Current experiments only consider the synthetic label noise with simple noise generation regime. It is interesting to see whether the findings hold on real-world human annotations, e.g., CIFAR-N [R7].

[R1] Liu Y, Guo H. Peer loss functions: Learning from noisy labels without knowing noise rates. ICML 2020.

[R2] Cheng H, Zhu Z, Li X, Gong Y, Sun X, Liu Y. Learning with instance-dependent label noise: A sample sieve approach. ICLR 2021.

[R3] Feng L, Shu S, Lin Z, Lv F, Li L, An B. Can cross entropy loss be robust to label noise? IJCAI 2021.

[R4] Lukasik M, Bhojanapalli S, Menon A, Kumar S. Does label smoothing mitigate label noise? ICML 2020.

[R5] Wei J, Liu H, Liu T, Niu G, Sugiyama M, Liu Y. To Smooth or Not? When Label Smoothing Meets Noisy Labels. ICML 2022.

[R6] Liu T, Tao D. Classification with noisy labels by importance reweighting. IEEE TPAMI 2015.

[R7] Wei J, Zhu Z, Cheng H, Liu T, Niu G, Liu Y. Learning with noisy labels revisited: A study using real-world human annotations. ICLR 2022.

Limitations:
They discussed the limitations in Sec. 3.1 Potential negative societal impacts are not applicable.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
$\textsf{Strengths:}$
1. This work connects several popular robust loss designs to sample-weighting curriculum.

2. Empirically, the authors explain the two open questions in the literature of learning-with-noisy-labels. The introduce of marginal effective learning rate looks interesting and helps with explaining the underfitting issue. And the shifting of soft-margin mitigates the underfitting, especially when the number of classes is large.

$\textsf{Weaknesses:}$

1. Presentation could be further improved, i.e., notation definitions, and argument statements  (please refer to Section "Questions" for more details.)

2. A potential weakness of this work is that all the analysis focuses on the class-dependent label noise, although it enables easiness of theoretical analysis, it was shown that real-world label noise tends to be instance-dependent [1]. And the majority section of this work falls into empirical explorations. It would be interesting to see whether the conclusions still hold for real-world noisy data, either small-scaled ones (CIFAR-10N, CIFAR-100N real-world noisy CIFAR [2]) or the large-scale Clothing 1M dataset.

$\textsf{References:}$

1. Part-dependent Label Noise: Towards Instance-dependent Label Noise. [NeurIPS 2020]

2. Learning with Noisy Labels Revisited: A Study Using Real-World Human Annotations. [ICLR 2022]

3. Learning From Massive Noisy Labeled Data for Image Classification. [CVPR 2015]

Summary:
When learning with noisy labels under certain robust loss designs, the authors focus on addressing two open questions: (1) Why do robust losses underfit? and (2) What affects the robustness of a loss? The two questions are explained through the view of sample-weighting curriculum perspective for certain robust losses. To overcome the underfitting issue, the authors propose to adapt the sample-weighting curriculums. To make loss more robust, the authors further show that adjusting the learning rate schedule helps.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
$\textsf{Strengths:}$
1. This work connects several popular robust loss designs to sample-weighting curriculum.

2. Empirically, the authors explain the two open questions in the literature of learning-with-noisy-labels. The introduce of marginal effective learning rate looks interesting and helps with explaining the underfitting issue. And the shifting of soft-margin mitigates the underfitting, especially when the number of classes is large.

$\textsf{Weaknesses:}$

1. Presentation could be further improved, i.e., notation definitions, and argument statements  (please refer to Section "Questions" for more details.)

2. A potential weakness of this work is that all the analysis focuses on the class-dependent label noise, although it enables easiness of theoretical analysis, it was shown that real-world label noise tends to be instance-dependent [1]. And the majority section of this work falls into empirical explorations. It would be interesting to see whether the conclusions still hold for real-world noisy data, either small-scaled ones (CIFAR-10N, CIFAR-100N real-world noisy CIFAR [2]) or the large-scale Clothing 1M dataset.

$\textsf{References:}$

1. Part-dependent Label Noise: Towards Instance-dependent Label Noise. [NeurIPS 2020]

2. Learning with Noisy Labels Revisited: A Study Using Real-World Human Annotations. [ICLR 2022]

3. Learning From Massive Noisy Labeled Data for Image Classification. [CVPR 2015]

Questions:
My concerns/questions/suggestions are listed below:

1. In Line 62, a pointer/reference to the argument "With $\eta<(k-1)<k, L$ is robust against symmetric label noise" would be appreciated.

2. In Line 64, $\ell$ is never defined, also for $\Delta p$ in Eqn. (2).

3. Typo: Line 108 "a sampled is learnt".

4. In Table 3, although it seems that MAE has pretty bad performances on the mentioned tasks, would fine-tuning influence much on the performance of MAE? Since 1.00 accuracy on CIFAR-100 is merely random guessing or predicting all samples to be the same class.

Limitations:
See Weakness 2.
